dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 56-year-old male with chest pain at the clinic in [CITY].
Hello, I'm Alex with EMS. What brought you to call 911?
Sharp chest pain, 8/10, and shortness of breath started 30 minutes ago. It radiates to my neck.
You're alert and oriented. Pupils are equal and reactive, Glasgow Coma Scale 15.
Airway is patent with no signs of obstruction.
You're breathing with full depth and no signs of obstructed air movement.
Your radial pulses are strong, equal, and regular. Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Your pulse is 100 beats per minute.
Respirations are at 20 per minute with full depth.
Blood pressure is 140/87 mmHg.
Oxygen saturation is 100%.
Have you had any additional symptoms besides the chest pain and shortness of breath?
I've been feeling ill over the weekend and had some nausea this morning.
Blood glucose is 314 mg/dL, which is elevated.
We will now apply 4-lead ECG monitoring.
We're performing a 12-lead ECG for more detailed cardiac evaluation.
The EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
I'll administer 324 mg of aspirin for the chest pain.
I'm starting your IV in your left antecubital vein for medication or fluid administration.
Your skin is warm, dry with normal turgor and no signs of rash or trauma.
Your pupils are PERRL (equal, round, reactive to light), 3 mm.
No signs of swelling, deformity, or bleeding in the neck area.
Do you have any medication allergies?
I am allergic to sulfonamides and metformin.
Could you tell me the medications you take regularly?
I take aspirin, carvedilol, lisinopril, insulin, and others for kidney and immune function.
You mentioned a kidney issue. Could you describe your medical history?
I have a history of heart failure, heart attack, stroke, and non-insulin-dependent diabetes. I had a kidney transplant in April 2019.
Pressing your abdomen doesn't reveal pain or abnormalities.
You can move and have normal strength in both arms and legs.
Chest movement is symmetrical with no signs of trauma or bruising.
I notice you have an AV fistula in your right arm. How long has that been there?
It's been present for a few months following my kidney transplant.
Got a complete list of your conditions, medications, and history for the hospital.
No contusions, deformities, or swelling in your arms and legs.
Capillary refill is under 2 seconds in both hands and feet.
Your eyes track smoothly with no signs of visual deficits.
I was told I have a UTI this morning and have had painful urination for a few days.
The nausea started on Sunday. Did you drink or eat any food before symptoms began?
I drank something about 2 to 3 hours ago but haven't eaten.
Rechecking vitals. Blood pressure is now 143/82 mmHg.
EKG remains the same with sinus tachycardia.
Has the pain decreased or changed in the last few minutes?
It's still 8/10 and radiates to my neck, especially on movement.
Glucose is still elevated at 314 mg/dL.
You also take anti-inflammatory drugs, muscle relaxants, and antiemetics. Were those mentioned earlier?
Yes, I take those for pain, nausea, and immune function after my transplant.
IV fluids will help with hydration and medication delivery if needed during transport.
We'll transport you in a semi-Fowler's position for safety and comfort. I'll secure the stretcher now.
[COMPANY_NAME] has your bed ready with resuscitation staff on standby.
Now helping you onto the stretcher in the semi-Fowler's position as discussed.
Securing you with a shoulder and lap belt for safe transport.
I'll give the hospital a complete pre-arrival report including all findings and medications.
Final vitals: 100 bpm, 20 rpm, 142/83 mmHg, 100% spo2, sinus tach EKG.
Handover to the hospital has been documented and care transition is in progress.
You are now being transferred to the emergency department at [COMPANY_NAME]. Stay safe.
I'll monitor the IV and your condition during transport to ensure no new developments.
Final report has been submitted with detailed notes for hospital staff.
